reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: documented persistence of an ICT/STEM skilled-labour shortage in Hungary (OECD Skills Outlook country notes; sectoral employer surveys reporting unfilled digital/technical vacancies) implies an interest in evidence about whether current selection actually grows the skilled-graduate pipeline or merely sorts by SES without adding skill
reactions.interest: employers and the tech/ICT sector seeking a reliable, growing pipeline of STEM- and digitally-skilled graduates
reactions.public_good_frame: national economic competitiveness and innovation capacity depend on the size and quality of the future skilled workforce, not only on equity outcomes
reactions.argument: An audit is only useful to this interest if it measures workforce-relevant outcomes (STEM/digital skill acquisition, not just SES composition) alongside equity; otherwise another 1-2 cohorts pass through an unexamined system while the sector's shortage worsens. A firm deadline is welcome only if it is actually enforced, since indefinite deferral leaves the pipeline question unresolved indefinitely.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would move to support if the audit's terms of reference explicitly include measurement of STEM/digital skill outcomes and labour-market destinations, and the statutory deadline is paired with a credible enforcement mechanism; would move to oppose if the audit scope excludes skill/labour-market metrics or the deadline is widely expected to be extended without consequence.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: documented employer concern that the current admission system screens on tutoring access and cultural capital rather than aptitude (general skills-shortage literature and OECD analyses of exam-prep-driven selection) implies an interest in widening the pool of students identified for advanced STEM/analytic tracks, provided identification of genuine aptitude is not weakened
reactions.interest: employers and the tech sector wanting a larger, more representative pool of high-aptitude students entering rigorous tracks, not a narrower or lower-quality one
reactions.public_good_frame: expanding access to rigorous tracks among currently excluded lower-SES and rural talent increases the total future supply of skilled workers available to the economy
reactions.argument: A pilot that broadens access to selective tracks without diluting the identification of genuine STEM/analytic aptitude would be a net gain for the future skilled-labour pool. But the scenario itself flags that a 'less-coachable' assessment design is unvalidated for whether it still identifies real aptitude versus merely different demographic groups; if the redesigned assessment fails to predict subsequent achievement, the sector loses a reliable aptitude signal without gaining a larger effective talent pool.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support scale-up if pilot evaluation shows the new assessment identifies students who go on to strong STEM/analytic outcomes at rates comparable to or better than the current exam, across a wider SES range; would oppose rollout if evaluation shows the redesigned assessment is a weaker predictor of subsequent skill development or is easily gamed by moderately-resourced families rather than reaching the intended pool.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: documented, strongly evidenced systemic teacher shortage in mathematics and sciences (OECD Education at a Glance; PSZ/PDSZ surveys cited in the scenario) implies an interest in not shrinking specialized STEM-track capacity without a credible substitute source of advanced STEM instruction
reactions.interest: employers and the tech sector dependent on a steady annual output of advanced-STEM-prepared secondary graduates feeding tertiary STEM/ICT programs
reactions.public_good_frame: the digital economy needs continuity of a skilled-graduate pipeline; disruption during a phase-down risks a multi-year dip in advanced STEM output precisely when shortages are already acute
reactions.argument: Reducing selective-track places is only equity-neutral for the skills pipeline if general schools receiving retained high-achieving pupils have adequate specialist STEM/digital-subject teaching capacity to sustain advanced instruction — and the scenario's own cost and political-risk sections concede this compensatory capacity is neither currently funded nor guaranteed to survive fiscal consolidation. Absent that guarantee, phase-down risks narrowing the advanced-STEM pipeline exactly where the shortage is worst.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support if compensatory capacity funding for general-school STEM/digital instruction is legislated as durable (not merely announced) and monitoring shows advanced-track STEM output is maintained through the transition; would oppose if compensatory funding lapses or if non-state maintainers absorbing displaced demand are the only source of continued advanced STEM instruction, since that would fragment rather than secure the pipeline.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: documented, strongly evidenced systemic teacher shortage in mathematics and sciences in Hungary (OECD Education at a Glance; PSZ/PDSZ surveys, as cited directly in the scenario's own political-risk section) implies opposition to a structural reform whose own implementation plan concedes full teacher-capacity buildout within a feasible timeline is unlikely
reactions.interest: employers and the tech sector requiring an uninterrupted, growing supply of advanced-STEM-prepared graduates for tertiary STEM/ICT pathways
reactions.public_good_frame: national competitiveness in a digital economy depends on sustained throughput of advanced STEM/technical talent; the scenario's own uncertainty section acknowledges this throughput is put at multi-year risk by the reform
reactions.argument: The scenario's own assumptions section states that Hungary building comparable teacher capacity for heterogeneous comprehensive classrooms within a feasible timeframe is 'flagged as unrealistic on a fixed short timeline given the scenario's own capacity evidence.' A structural abolition of specialized selective tracks, undertaken while the specialist STEM teacher shortage is already severe and unresolved, risks a multi-year disruption to advanced STEM/digital skill formation with no credible bridging mechanism specified for the transition period.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would reconsider if a credible, funded teacher-capacity buildout (recruitment plus retraining) is demonstrably sequenced to precede or run well ahead of classroom restructuring in STEM/digital subjects specifically, and if advanced-track options for high-aptitude students are preserved within the comprehensive model rather than eliminated outright.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: documented evidence that Portugal achieved system-level PISA gains without altering its selection age (OECD PISA trend reports; Crato 2020, as cited in the scenario) implies this approach can be read as consistent with an interest in raising the general-school skill floor while preserving the existing selective-track pipeline that currently feeds advanced STEM/tertiary study
reactions.interest: employers and the tech sector wanting both a preserved elite STEM pipeline and a higher basic digital/numeracy floor across the wider workforce entering non-selective tracks
reactions.public_good_frame: a digital economy needs depth at the top (specialist STEM talent from existing selective tracks) and breadth at the base (general workforce digital/numeracy competence); this scenario is the only one of the five that targets both without disrupting the existing selective pipeline
reactions.argument: Retaining the 6/8-year structure preserves the current supply channel for advanced STEM talent while TEIP-style targeted funding and curriculum standards could raise general-track numeracy and digital literacy, widening the base of digitally competent workers the sector also needs for non-specialist technical roles. The scenario's own risk section, however, flags that this targeted funding is a plausible early casualty of fiscal consolidation, which would hollow out the general-track benefit while leaving the equity-legitimizing effect of unchanged selection in place.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support is conditional on the TEIP-style funding being legislated with multi-year or ring-fenced durability rather than annual discretion; would withdraw support if funding is not made durable, since that would leave the selective STEM pipeline intact while delivering no offsetting gain in general-track digital/numeracy competence.
